---
title: "Week 1: Computer is Friend & What is Code"
number-sections: true
number-depth: 2
---



This week we're going to learn more about the building blocks of the R 
programming language. 

In total, we have:

#### đź“– Readings: 60 min

#### đź’» Activities: 30-minutes

#### đź“˝ Videos: 0 min 

## R is Friend

::: callout
## Learning Objectives

+ Identify three of the foundational data types in R
+ Explain similarities and differences between different object types
+ Use indexing to extract elements from different object types
+ Use functions to calculate summary statistics
+ Use logical values and indexing to calculate summary statistics
:::

### đź“– Required Reading: [R is Friend](
https://r-is-your-friend.github.io/course-pack/02-r-is-friend.html)

There are **a lot** of check-in questions this week! Each section contains 1-3 
questions targeting the ideas covered in that section. We encourage you to 
keep track of your answers as you progress!

::: {.callout-check-in}

For each of the examples below, identify what data type do you believe the
vector will have.

1. `c(67, 13, 1989, TRUE)`
2. `c("hello", "goodbye", TRUE)`
3. `c("TRUE", FALSE, TRUE, "FALSE")`

4. Draw out how this matrix will look:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
shows <- c("Tale of a Thousand Stars", 
           "Bad Buddy", 
           "Moonlight Chicken", 
           "Not Me")

matrix(shows, ncol = 2)
```
:::



::: {.cell}

:::


5. What type of object is this?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[[1]]
[1] "Malus domestica"     "Citrus sinensis"     "Citrus limon"       
[4] "Citrus aurantifolia" "Mangifera indica"    "Prunus persica"     

[[2]]
[1]  3  5 12 12  5  3
```


:::
:::


6. What type of object is this?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     [,1] [,2]
[1,]    3   12
[2,]    5    5
[3,]   12    3
```


:::
:::


7. What type of object is this?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
  fruits               trees
1  apple     Malus domestica
2 orange     Citrus sinensis
3  lemon        Citrus limon
4   lime Citrus aurantifolia
5  mango    Mangifera indica
6  peach      Prunus persica
```


:::
:::


8. What type of object is this?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
$tree
[1] "Malus domestica"     "Citrus sinensis"     "Citrus limon"       
[4] "Citrus aurantifolia" "Mangifera indica"    "Prunus persica"     

$harvest_window
[1]  3  5 12 12  5  3
```


:::
:::


9. What type of object is this?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Malus domestica"     "Citrus sinensis"     "Citrus limon"       
[4] "Citrus aurantifolia" "Mangifera indica"    "Prunus persica"     
```


:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
fruits <- c("apple",
            "orange", 
            "lemon", 
            "lime", 
            "mango", 
            "peach")
```
:::


10. Grab the first three elements of the vector named `fruits`.

11. Grab the first, third, and fifth element of the vector named `fruits`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
trees <- c("Malus domestica", 
           "Citrus sinensis", 
           "Citrus limon", 
           "Citrus aurantifolia", 
           "Mangifera indica", 
           "Prunus persica")

tree_matrix <- matrix(trees, ncol = 2)
tree_matrix
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     [,1]              [,2]                 
[1,] "Malus domestica" "Citrus aurantifolia"
[2,] "Citrus sinensis" "Mangifera indica"   
[3,] "Citrus limon"    "Prunus persica"     
```


:::
:::


12. Grab the tree in the second row, second column of the matrix named 
`tree_matrix`.

13. Grab the trees in the first two rows of the matrix named `tree_matrix`. 

14. Grab the trees in the second column of the matrix named `tree_matrix`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
harvest_window <- c(3, 5, 12, 12, 5, 3)

fruit_tree_harvest <- list(fruit = fruits, 
                           tree = trees, 
                           harvest_window = harvest_window)
fruit_tree_harvest
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
$fruit
[1] "apple"  "orange" "lemon"  "lime"   "mango"  "peach" 

$tree
[1] "Malus domestica"     "Citrus sinensis"     "Citrus limon"       
[4] "Citrus aurantifolia" "Mangifera indica"    "Prunus persica"     

$harvest_window
[1]  3  5 12 12  5  3
```


:::
:::


15. Grab the first component (`fruit`) of the list named `fruit_tree_harvest`.  

16. Grab the third tree from the `tree` component of the list named  
`fruit_tree_harvest`.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
fruit_trees <- data.frame(fruits, trees, harvest_window)
fruit_trees
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
  fruits               trees harvest_window
1  apple     Malus domestica              3
2 orange     Citrus sinensis              5
3  lemon        Citrus limon             12
4   lime Citrus aurantifolia             12
5  mango    Mangifera indica              5
6  peach      Prunus persica              3
```


:::
:::


17. Grab the second column (`trees`) of the `fruit_trees` dataframe.

18. Grab the second and third columns (`trees`, `harvest_window`) of the
`fruit_trees` dataframe.

19. Grab the second row of all three columns of the `fruit_trees` dataframe.

20. What will be the output of the following operation?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
vec1 <- c(1, 2, 3, 4)
vec2 <- c(5, 6, 7, 8)

vec1 + vec2
```
:::


(a) `1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8`
(b) `6 8 10 12`
(c) this code will have an error
:::
